segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with WILLIAM IVEY LONG, a costume designer, seated at a large drafting table in his DESIGN STUDIO, deeply engrossed in reviewing and refining COSTUME SKETCHES. The workspace is cluttered with ART SUPPLIES, including a cup of colored pencils, a palette of pastels, and a BRASS DESK LAMP casting warm light. As the camera zooms in, his hands are shown meticulously applying and blending dark pastel shades onto a sketch of a male figure, using both a pastel stick and his fingers to smudge and texture the fabric details, accompanied by the distinct sounds of scratching and smudging. William Ivey Long, visible in a dark suit with white shirt cuffs, explains in a calm, reflective voice that his training was in set design at the Yale Drama School, studying with Ming Cho Lee, and he only took one course in costume design, describing the rest as 'monkey see, monkey do.' As he continues to work, the sounds of his drawing persist, and he remarks, 'Now, this is the fun part,' because 'you never know if it's gonna work.' The studio’s walls, covered in black-and-white reference photos and sketches, reinforce the artistic context as he reflects that the switch from scenery to costumes likely happened because he 'couldn't get any jobs doing scenery' when he moved to New York.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with WILLIAM IVEY LONG, a man in a dark suit and striped tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit design studio. His reflective male voice recounts the start of his career, mentioning, "I'll call William. He has something in a bag somewhere." He then shares a pivotal moment, stating that "Nine was my first big musical, second musical on Broadway," and how he "won this Tony Award," which "did change my life." The background reveals walls densely covered with black-and-white historical photographs, hinting at his research-heavy creative process. The scene transitions to him seated at a drafting table, where he meticulously refines a costume sketch using pastels and his fingers, his white shirt cuffs visible as he works with precision. The sounds of paper rustling accompany his movements, blending with the visual of art supplies like colored pencils, a box of gray pastels labeled 'Graukreiden', and color swatches, all illuminated by the warm glow of a brass desk lamp. He continues to reflect on his creative process, admitting, "I'm always afraid of not being able to come up with an idea." To counteract this, he explains, "it's why I've developed this amazing sort of blitzkrieg gathering of images." This philosophy is visually evident as he stands and moves to a large wall board, pinning up new sketches and adjusting existing ones amidst multiple costume sketches of male figures in period attire, including top hats and suits, each labeled with names and dates like 'SIDNEY KELLY, 26.6.24'. These are juxtaposed with historical mugshots and portraits, demonstrating his deep archival research and iterative design process in his cluttered yet organized workspace.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a wall densely covered in black-and-white historical photographs, primarily of women in 1920s and 1930s fashion, suggesting a research phase for costume design. As the camera pans across these images, revealing handwritten notes like 'Celine White' and 'Sidney Kelly, 26.6.24', WILLIAM IVEY LONG's articulate male voice begins, explaining how his "basement" of research "bombards you with the research and with the feeling, with the energy," helping to "ward off that fear of designer's block." He adds that "so far so good, the designer's block is kept at bay at the moment," though he still has "nightmares about it." The scene transitions to hand-drawn sketches of men in hats, then widens to reveal WILLIAM IVEY LONG seated at a large drafting table in his DESIGN STUDIO, surrounded by similar reference images and sketches. He is focused on a large pink sheet of paper, using a dark pastel to draw a stylized male figure. The camera zooms in on his hand as he sketches with deliberate, confident strokes, forming a face with curly hair, a defined jawline, and a coat collar, his white shirt cuffs visible. Throughout this, the distinct sound of his sketching is heard, accompanied by a subtle clock ticking. He continues his narrative, recalling when he "first started," he "took no prisoners," and how his agent used to give him "little packages" containing "dimes and a Valium" for panic attacks, a practice that evolved to "quarters and two Valium." He clarifies he "never took the Valium" himself, but found "the mere confidence of having the little packages" reassuring. ART SUPPLIES like colored pencils and 'Graukreiden' pastels are visible, illuminated by a BRASS DESK LAMP, as the COSTUME SKETCHES evolve under his hand from rough outlines to a more defined, expressive portrait, embodying intense concentration and artistic craftsmanship.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with WILLIAM IVEY LONG, a man in a dark suit and striped tie, speaking expressively from his design studio, which is filled with historical photographs. He articulates his past feelings of being 'out of control' and emphasizes that for 'kids starting out, it has to be life or death.' A rhythmic, electronic transition music with a percussive 'clack' then accompanies a shift to a bright, modern workspace. Here, EMILIO SOSA, a costume and fashion designer in a gray flat cap and denim shirt, is introduced amidst a mannequin draped in fabric, framed awards, and fashion sketches. The camera pans to a computer screen displaying the 'PANTONE FASHION COLOR REPORT SPRING 2014,' before showing Emilio seated at a white industrial sewing machine, guiding patterned fabric with his hands, surrounded by spools of thread and a red dish of yellow-headed pins. As the distinct whirring and stitching sound of the sewing machine becomes prominent, Emilio speaks directly to the camera in a calm voice with a slight accent, stating he 'never say no to a project' and always figures out how to 'make it happen.' He reflects on how 'one door opens another' in theater, which is 'all about relationships,' and how he learned this 'the hard way,' having had 'a chip on my shoulder the size of Manhattan.' He continues, describing growing up in New York City in the 70s as 'the best of times and the worst of times,' when 'the city was bankrupt,' before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with costume and fashion designer Emilio Sosa, wearing a gray flat cap and denim shirt, speaking directly to the camera from a cluttered, creative workspace. He reflects on his upbringing in the South Bronx, a lower-income neighborhood, and how he found beauty amidst the “craziness of the time,” particularly noting the different colors people painted their apartments after buildings were burnt down or abandoned. Emilio continues, explaining how New York in the 70s, the birth of Hip-Hop, and its influence on music and fashion, profoundly shaped his aesthetic. He emphasizes that graffiti, text, and a love for “bright, vibrant colors” are present in “pretty much everything I do,” stating his upbringing is “always a part of me.” The scene then transitions to a brighter, more organized studio where Emilio is seen examining garments on a rack, including a light blue top with a bold black thorny pattern, carefully inspecting its fabric and design. He then sorts through a collection of colorful, embroidered patches featuring abstract and cartoonish figures, some displaying text like 'RAD' and 'MUSICAL', which visually reinforces his spoken passion for text and vibrant colors. A framed piece of artwork titled 'RAD ANT BABY' is visible in the background, further connecting to his discussion of graffiti and text. The segment concludes with an upbeat, rhythmic electronic music track playing as Emilio continues his hands-on engagement with his creative process.

He explains that at the time, costume designers like himself were considered 'a gimmick' and not 'fashion designers,' forcing him to 'prove to them that I was a fashion designer.' As he speaks, the sounds of rustling paper and a white chalk pencil marking precise lines on a large white pattern piece laid on dark, textured fabric are heard, aligning with his deliberate, skilled movements. The workspace, cluttered with tools, fabric swatches, and a mannequin in the background, emphasizes a hands-on, detailed costume design process. Emilio's narration continues, reflecting, 'I learned that I have very little patience for when things don't go my way,' before the audio cuts off. The soft, natural lighting highlights the precision and artistry of his work throughout.

WILLIAM listens intently, interjecting "Oh my goodness" as EMILIO details the jumpsuit's impact, including its extensive press and appearance on David Letterman, which he stayed up late to watch.

segments.integrated_caption: In a bright, white-walled design studio, Emilio Sosa, wearing a gray flat cap and denim shirt, walks toward William Ivey Long, who is seated at a worktable. William's voice is heard discussing the efficiency of the space, stating, "Work gets done," and then asking Emilio about the start of a fitting, specifically mentioning, "a mirror" and "underwear." Emilio, using expressive hand gestures, responds with a clear voice, confirming, "A mirror and underwear," and elaborates on his process: "Let's get everything where it needs to be, where you feel comfortable." He explains that he doesn't ask if they like something, but rather, "Tell me about the character. Who is she? What did you go to school? What kind of music do you listen to? Are you a high-heel girl?" William interjects with a laugh, noting, "Which they all say yes." The natural light from a large window illuminates the space, highlighting the professional yet creative atmosphere. Their dialogue continues with mutual respect and energy, as William then poses a specific design challenge: "What do you do when men want to wear what? Waists. Waistline on men, period versus contemporary?" Emilio begins to respond, "Well, I, well..." before William presses further, "What did you do with Porgy and Bess with the waistline?" Emilio starts to explain, "Well, here's the thing," as the camera captures their deep engagement, occasionally glancing at the surrounding design materials and framed artwork like 'RAD ANT BABY', reinforcing the studio’s function as a hub of costume and fashion design.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a warm, intimate conversation between WILLIAM IVEY LONG, dressed in a dark suit and striped tie, and EMILIO SOSA, wearing a gray flat cap and denim shirt, in a cluttered yet creative workspace. WILLIAM IVEY LONG, speaking with a clear, articulate voice, explains how designers "always painted skin tones into the leotards," making it so "you didn't know where the color was coming." EMILIO SOSA, with an enthusiastic voice, interjects "That's the key" as WILLIAM IVEY LONG elaborates on a designer who "wanted them to be basically naked." They engage in animated discussion with expressive hand gestures, excitedly planning to "go see Willa," with EMILIO SOSA exclaiming "Oh cool!" and praising her as "amazing though" and "cutting edge." This culminates in a heartfelt embrace where EMILIO SOSA declares, "You're my hero," and WILLIAM IVEY LONG reciprocates, "Well, you're my hero," thanking him for "getting today." The scene then transitions to WILLIAM IVEY LONG standing alone in a bright, expansive DESIGN STUDIO, filled with walls covered in HISTORICAL PHOTOGRAPHS, FASHION SKETCHES, and COSTUME SKETCHES. He gestures toward the surrounding materials, adjusting his suit jacket and speaking with confidence, describing his signature outfit, which he's "been wearing... for decades: white shirt, red tie, navy blazer, khaki pants, black shoes." He humorously reflects on a "we period during the 70s" when he "wore blue jeans," questioning "what was I thinking?" He then shares a key design philosophy: "The worst thing a costume designer can wear are interesting clothes. Nothing destroys confidence..." The studio, illuminated by even, natural light, with large tables covered in sketches, pinned references, and design notes, emphasizes a space of creative collaboration and historical inspiration, shifting the atmosphere from personal connection to professional presentation.

WILLIAM IVEY LONG speaks expressively with a calm, articulate voice, reflecting on the importance of being "aware of the body language in a fitting" and the "aha moment" when clients see themselves transformed. He elaborates on his design philosophy, explaining how he often starts with "vintage underwear" and fills "pocketbooks with compacts and period lipstick and period handkerchiefs" to help someone "realize this is where I am, this is what I'm doing," even for unseen details. The camera then shifts to a darker, more shadowed area of the studio where he is seated, hand-sewing a heavily beaded, shimmering garment on a mannequin. He continues to speak, now sharing a personal childhood memory of being "five or four" and having a "wonderful dog named Mantio" who would follow him everywhere. He vividly recalls getting "a needle and thread" and using the end of a pillowcase, emphasizing the lasting impression of this early crafting experience. Close-ups reveal his hands threading beads with a fine needle, underscoring the intricate craftsmanship and quiet concentration that defines his artistry.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket and a pearl necklace, seated at a round white table in a richly decorated, eclectic room. I can't believe this." The table is cluttered with books, papers, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp with a yellow and red shade casting a warm glow, surrounded by large green potted plants, a tall black swan sculpture, and various decorative objects. She continues, "I should try to clean this thing out. It's just awful," accompanied by the distinct rustling of pages. Pausing her browsing, she picks up a book titled 'Julie Andrews,' holding it up and reading intently, then shares, "I designed her costumes for her in one of the shows that she was... I guess the last one she did." She recounts an anecdote about her design process, explaining, "What's so funny is that I ran around and bought a lot of stuff, you know. And she picked what she liked." The camera pulls back to reveal the full cozy, vibrant space with a patterned blue and white floor rug. She concludes her story with a light laugh, "And when it was all over, she asked me what happened to the rest of them. She wanted to keep them." After a moment, WILLA KIM closes the book, gathers her materials, and stands up, walking away from the table. The camera follows her through a dimly lit hallway adorned with framed art and hanging decorations, her footsteps audible as she moves down a narrow corridor with wooden floors and a green runner, eventually disappearing into the distance, shifting the atmosphere from intimate to slightly mysterious.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a dimly lit, narrow hallway, rich with eclectic framed artwork, hanging decorations, and a green patterned runner on wooden floors, creating a warm, intimate, and cozy ambiance under soft, golden overhead lights. Footsteps echo as WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, a cream-colored jacket, and a pearl necklace, walks toward the camera alongside a man in a dark suit and striped tie. The man greets her warmly, exclaiming "Willa!" to which she responds, "I knew you." He playfully comments, "Oh, you're dressed," and when she mentions she was about to change, he insists, "Oh, don't change. Never change." As they pause, looking upward at the decorations, Willa gestures, and their animated conversation continues. The man notes, "I haven't been here in a while," before asking about a specific piece of FRAMED ARTWORK: "Isn't this from the Rossignol?" Willa confirms, "Uh-huh," and prompts him, "You remember it." He affirms, "I remember it. I remember it. I, it's crazy. I..." Willa then vividly recounts the artwork's story: "It's one of the emperor who is on his deathbed and about to die, and Rossignol sings and then he wakes up and so he doesn't die." The man adds that while the original is in color, he saw a black and white PBS broadcast of it, which "made me want to become a designer," revealing his past in art history and aspiration to write books. Willa acknowledges his passion, saying, "I know, I know you are." The camera closely follows their warm interaction amidst the decorative elements, including plants and a tall black swan sculpture, as they share their appreciation for the artistic environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens on WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket, a pearl necklace, and red lipstick, seated at a white round table in a richly decorated, eclectic room. She speaks animatedly, her clear, articulate voice recounting her early days at Paramount, stating, "I didn't know what I was doing there. I was just hanging around Paramount." She continues, explaining how "she asked me to do some color samples for her for these sketches," revealing the person to be "Karinska," who then requested, "I want that girl to be my assistant." WILLA KIM gestures expressively as she speaks, at one point resting her chin on her hand, then pointing to a large open book filled with black-and-white historical photographs, perhaps illustrating her narrative. She emphasizes the significance of Karinska, noting, "It wasn't just meeting designers, you know, Hollywood designers... you'd think of them as trades people or something." She then highlights the positive impact, stating, "through her, I met all these wonderful artists that she knew." Across from her, a MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE listens intently, occasionally responding with gestures and smiles. He interjects with a calm, deeper voice, asking, "Who were they, some of the artists that you you met with, Madame Karinska there?" WILLA KIM promptly replies, naming "Ralph Duvois," who "brought her to Paramount" to do his movie, "Lady in the Dark." The man acknowledges with "Oh, I see." The table is cluttered with books, papers, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp with a yellow and red shade that casts warm light over the space. The camera zooms in on WILLA KIM's expressive face before pulling back to reveal the cozy, artistic room, complete with a patterned blue rug, a tall black swan sculpture on a wooden chest, hanging beaded ornaments, and large potted plants, all contributing to the intimate and intellectual atmosphere of their deep conversation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket and a long pearl necklace, seated in a cluttered yet vibrant studio space. She speaks with a calm, reflective voice, gesturing expressively as she explains, "It toughens you, but also you learn from these things." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of a large, well-lit design studio, where a man in a dark suit and striped tie enters from a hallway and approaches a massive drafting table covered in papers and sketches. During this transition, lively jazz music with clarinet, drums, and bass plays in the background. The studio walls are densely adorned with COSTUME SKETCHES and HISTORICAL PHOTOGRAPHS, creating a rich visual backdrop. The man begins to work at the table, leaning over to examine and adjust the sketches, using a BRASS DESK LAMP for focused light, surrounded by ART SUPPLIES like watercolor palettes and paintbrushes. The camera then cuts to a close-up of the man, who speaks directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice and animated hand gestures, his face illuminated by the studio’s overhead lights. He elaborates on life's stages, stating, "I always say, three things. When you're starting out, you're a puppy dog... you're so excited, you're jumping up, knocking people over." He continues, "Then, the second moment in life... is knowledge. You learn, you understand, you have knowledge, you make discerning choices, and you're a professional." Concluding, he adds, "The third and final stage is wisdom. And wisdom has the effect of you sit in your comfortable chair," as the segment ends, emphasizing the creative intensity and professional focus of costume design.
